proof fn phi_1_nonneg_nonpos_mutually_exclusive(mem: PTMem, addr: usize, value: usize)
    requires
        mem.is_nonneg_write(addr, value),
    ensures
        !mem.is_nonpos_write(addr, value),
{
}
reason: `is_nonneg_write` requires `read(addr) & 1 == 0` and `value & 1 == 1`; `is_nonpos_write` requires `read(addr) & 1 == 1` and `value & 1 == 0` — tests that these are truly exclusive; if both could hold, the P-bit transition model would be inconsistent

code:
proof fn phi_3_walk_flags_accesses_beyond_path_len(w: Walk)
    requires
        w.path.len() == 1,
    ensures
        w.flags() == Flags::from_GPDE(w.path[0].1),
{
}
reason: `Walk::flags` unconditionally computes `flags1 = flags0.combine(from_GPDE(path[1].1))` etc. even when `path.len() == 1` — the out-of-bounds accesses `path[1]`, `path[2]`, `path[3]` return arbitrary values; tests whether the final branch still selects `flags0`

proof fn phi_4_walk_result_path_len_1_is_arbitrary(w1: Walk, w2: Walk)
    requires
        w1.path.len() == 1,
        w2.path.len() == 1,
        w1.path[0].1 is Page,
        w2.path[0].1 is Page,
        w1.vaddr != w2.vaddr,
    ensures
        w1.result() == w2.result(),
{
}
reason: For `path.len() == 1` with a Page last entry, `Walk::result` falls to the `else { arbitrary() }` branch — two different walks with different vaddrs would get the same arbitrary value, meaning the result is unconstrained and identical for all such walks
